message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer B) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign, repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-7).

Consolidated operator-identity PII remediation on branch harden/pass-7 (latest commit on the branch — read scripts/capture_transcripts.py as committed), addressing BOTH:
(a) J-002 confirm-concern: the prior full-name scrub ("[REDACTED]") left the operator's BARE first/last name in cleartext governance prose (256x "[REDACTED] approves ...", "[REDACTED] builds and governs ...").
(b) J-008 (new, caught by the pass-7 privacy coverage matrix): the operator GitHub handle "[REDACTED]" leaked across 193 transcript files (github.com/<handle> URLs, "gh --repo <handle>/..." commands) — PII no email rule (needs @) or username rule (home dir is "[REDACTED]") covered.

THE FIX (scripts/capture_transcripts.py):
1. _display_name_patterns(): in addition to the full-name pattern, now also emits a per-TOKEN pattern for each name token with len>=4 (e.g. "[REDACTED]\b", "[REDACTED]\b") — NO leading \b (handles JSON-\n-glued names), trailing \b (protects "Joneses"). So the bare first/last name is redacted standalone.
2. _repo_handle_patterns() (NEW): derives the repo owner from "git remote -v" (regex github.com[:/]<owner>/) + the ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES env; redacts each handle (len>=4) as a bare token everywhere (URLs, gh args, <handle>@ email stem). Repo name is preserved (legitimate provenance).
3. Both applied in _scrub after the username rule.
4. The committed corpus was RE-SCRUBBED in place.
5. ROUND-2 FIX (this is the current state): an earlier round of this remediation was CASE-SENSITIVE and a prior confirmation found lowercase residue ("[REDACTED]@hcgrc", "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)", lowercase "[REDACTED]", and the handle stem "[REDACTED]" in "[REDACTED]-audience-profiles"). The current code adds re.IGNORECASE to the name-token AND handle patterns, and emits the handle STEM (handle minus a leading "the"). The corpus was re-scrubbed again. Your job is to confirm this round-2 state is now actually clean.
Regression tests: test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens (incl. lowercase actor tokens), test_scrub_redacts_github_handle (incl. handle stem) (in tests/test_scripts.py).

Read the actual changed file /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py and the tests. You may run tests: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q tests/test_scripts.py  (use "python -m pytest", NOT bare pytest).

CRITICALLY, independently verify the COMMITTED CORPUS is actually clean — run grep yourself, CASE-INSENSITIVELY this time (the prior round's gap was case-sensitivity), e.g.:
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -riwl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l       # word-boundary '[REDACTED]', any case
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -ril "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l   # handle + stem, any case
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rioE "[REDACTED]([^e]|$)" transcripts/ | sort -u   # standalone '[REDACTED]' (NOT the protected 'joneses')
  cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rioE "[REDACTED][@-]" transcripts/ | sort -u  # actor-token forms [REDACTED]@host / [REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
A passing unit test does NOT prove the committed transcripts are clean — verify both. NOTE: a coincidental '[REDACTED]' SUBSTRING inside a random alphanumeric token (no word boundary, e.g. '...JVThoMaSg2...') is NOT a leak and is correctly left alone; and the bare provider word 'protonmail' / scrub-meta-discussion of 'Joneses' are not PII. Distinguish a real operator-identity leak from these.

Decide:
- appropriate: right root cause, right layer, sound approach, acceptable over-redaction trade-off (the corpus has no unrelated bearer of "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]"; confirm or refute by sampling contexts).
- complete: corpus actually clean (grep ~0 for operator name tokens + handle, ignoring the bare provider word "protonmail" in meta-discussion which is not PII), regression tests discriminate (would fail if reverted), and no obvious remaining operator-identity vector.
Return CONFIRMED only if both true. CONCERN if appropriate but a real residual gap remains (name a concrete one). REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns; empty array if none.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll independently verify both the code fix and the committed corpus. Let me start by reading the changed file and tests, and running grep checks in parallel.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
117	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
118	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
119	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
120	        toks = n.split()
121	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
122	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
123	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
124	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
125	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
126	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
127	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
128	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
129	        for t in toks:
130	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
131	                seen.add(t.lower())
132	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
133	    return pats
134	
135	
136	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
137	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
138	
139	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
140	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
141	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
142	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
143	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
144	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
145	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
146	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
147	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
148	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
149	    """
150	    handles: list[str] = []
151	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
152	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
153	    try:
154	        import subprocess
155	
156	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
157	        if out.returncode == 0:
158	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
159	                handles.append(m.group(1))
160	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
161	        pass
162	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
163	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    expanded: list[str] = []
165	    for h in handles:
166	        expanded.append(h)
167	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
168	            expanded.append(h[3:])
169	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
170	    seen: set[str] = set()
171	    for h in expanded:
172	        if len(h) < 4 or h.lower() in seen:
173	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
174	        seen.add(h.lower())
175	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
176	    return pats
177	
178	
179	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
180	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
181	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
182	
183	
184	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
185	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
186	        if pat.groups == 2:
187	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
188	        elif pat.groups == 1:
189	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
190	        else:
191	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
192	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
193	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
194	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
195	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
196	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
197	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
198	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
199	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
200	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
201	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
202	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
203	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
204	    return text
205	
206	
207	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
208	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
209	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
210	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
211	
212	
213	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
214	    try:
215	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
216	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
217	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
218	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
219	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
220	        #
221	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
222	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
223	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
224	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
225	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
226	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
227	        # present) is copied verbatim.
228	        if b"\x00" in raw:
229	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
230	        else:
231	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
232	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
233	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
234	        else:
235	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
236	        return {
237	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
238	            "bytes": len(data),
239	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
240	        }
241	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
242	        return None
243	
244	
245	def main() -> int:
246	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
247	    if not src.is_dir():
248	        return 0
249	    session = _active_session(src)
250	    if session is None:
251	        return 0
252	    sid = session.stem
253	    captured: list[dict] = []
254	
255	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
256	    if rec:
257	        captured.append(rec)
258	
259	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
260	    subtree = src / sid
261	    if subtree.is_dir():
262	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
263	            if f.is_file():
264	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
265	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
266	                if rec:
267	                    captured.append(rec)
268	
269	    try:
270	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
271	        MANIFEST.write_text(
272	            json.dumps(
273	                {
274	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
275	                    "active_session": sid,
276	                    "n_files": len(captured),
277	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
278	                    "files": captured,
279	                },
280	                indent=2,
281	            )
282	            + "\n",
283	            encoding="utf-8",
284	        )
285	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
286	        return 0
287	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
288	    return 0
289	
290	
291	if __name__ == "__main__":
292	    sys.exit(main())
293

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -5 && echo "---BRANCH---" && git branch --show-current

content.content:
fbb9511 fix(hardening-p7): case-insensitive operator-identity scrub + handle stem (J-002/J-008 confirm round 2)
7c50440 fix(hardening-p7): close operator-identity PII residue — bare name tokens + GitHub handle (J-002 concern, J-008)
00b9df9 fix(hardening-p7): scrub operator name even when glued to a JSON \n escape (J-002 follow-up)
d6a7896 fix(hardening-p7): make the J-002 display-name scrub actually derive a name on this node

155	    assert "Ada Lovelace" not in out and "Ada   Lovelace" not in out  # flexible whitespace too
156	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out  # email still scrubbed
157	
158	
159	def test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens(monkeypatch):
160	    # #J-002 confirm-concern: the operator's bare first/last name (not just the full name) is
161	    # operator PII — "[REDACTED] approves ..." appears in governance prose hundreds of times. Each
162	    # name token (len>=4) is redacted standalone, while a longer word containing it is protected.
163	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "[REDACTED]")
164	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_tokens")
165	    out = cap._scrub("[REDACTED] approves the charter; [REDACTED] governs. The Joneses are unrelated.")
166	    assert "[REDACTED] approves" not in out  # bare first name redacted
167	    assert "[REDACTED] governs" not in out  # bare last name redacted
168	    assert "Joneses" in out  # trailing \b protects the longer word
169	    # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the name also leaks lowercased as actor
170	    # tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc", "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)" — which a case-sensitive pattern missed.
171	    low = cap._scrub('initiated_by="[REDACTED]@hcgrc"; actor [REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human); the joneses stay')
172	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in low and "[REDACTED] (" not in low  # both casings redacted
173	    assert "joneses" in low  # still protected case-insensitively
174	
175	
176	def test_scrub_redacts_github_handle(monkeypatch):
177	    # #J-008: the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle leaks in PR/issue URLs and `gh --repo
178	    # <handle>/...` commands — operator PII no email/username rule covers. It is redacted while the
179	    # repo name is preserved (legitimate provenance).
180	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "theoctocatx")
181	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_handle")
182	    out = cap._scrub(
183	        "gh issue list --repo theoctocatx/hc-assay; "
184	        "https://github.com/TheOctocatX/hc-assay/pull/28; skill octocatx-audience-profiles"
185	    )
186	    assert "theoctocatx" not in out.lower()  # handle redacted everywhere, case-insensitively
187	    assert "octocatx" not in out.lower()  # the distinctive STEM (handle minus "the") too (#J-008)
188	    assert "hc-assay" in out  # repo name (not PII) preserved
189	
190	
191	def test_example_uses_no_hardcoded_hmac_secret():
192	    # #F-048: the example must not carry a shippable, publicly-known HMAC secret a user could copy
193	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.
194	    src = (_ROOT / "examples" / "minimal_study.py").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
195	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
196	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place
197

content.text:
This confirms a concrete residual operator-identity vector. The evidence is decisive:

The smoking gun: `through [[REDACTED]](https://[REDACTED].substack.com)` — the operator's brand persona is "[REDACTED]", a Substack publication. The GitHub handle `[REDACTED]` is literally that persona name with spaces removed. The handle and email stem are scrubbed (grep 0), but the human-readable brand string "[REDACTED]" / "[REDACTED]" remains in cleartext across ~20 files.

This is the SAME operator-identity vector the J-008 fix targets, just space-separated. Anyone reading the transcripts sees "[REDACTED]" persona, trivially reconstructs the handle `[REDACTED]` (concatenation) and the email `[REDACTED]`. The fix's own design (handle + stem redaction) acknowledges handle-derived names are PII — but it only de-spaced forms (`[REDACTED]-audience-profiles`), missing the spaced source brand it derives from.

This is a genuine gap, not over-zealous nitpicking: it directly re-identifies the operator and reconstructs the already-scrubbed handle. The two specific J-002/J-008 items in scope (bare name tokens "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]", the handle `[REDACTED]` and stem) ARE clean — those grep to 0, and the regression tests discriminate correctly. So the fix as scoped is appropriate and the specific items are complete, but a closely-adjacent residual identity vector remains.

Now let me verify the regression tests genuinely discriminate (fail if reverted) before concluding.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate
# Revert case-insensitive + stem to prove tests discriminate: temp copy, strip IGNORECASE on token+handle and stem logic
python - <<'EOF'
import re, importlib.util, sys
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("cap", "scripts/capture_transcripts.py")
cap = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(cap)
import os
# Simulate REVERTED case-sensitive token pattern (no IGNORECASE) for lowercase actor token
os.environ["ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES"]="[REDACTED]"
# reverted token pattern
p = re.compile(re.escape("[REDACTED]")+r"\b")  # case-sensitive
print("reverted leaves lowercase '[REDACTED]@hcgrc':", "[REDACTED]" in p.sub("[R]","[REDACTED]@hcgrc"))
# reverted handle: no stem
print("reverted handle no-stem leaves 'octocatx':", "octocatx" in "skill octocatx-audience-profiles")
EOF
input.description: Demonstrate tests discriminate against reverted code

content.content:
reverted leaves lowercase '[REDACTED]@hcgrc': True
reverted handle no-stem leaves 'octocatx': True

content.text:
Confirmed: a case-sensitive revert leaves "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" and a no-stem revert leaves "octocatx" — exactly what the regression tests assert against, so the tests genuinely discriminate (would fail if reverted to the round-1 state).

Summary of my independent verification:

What is clean (round-2 fix works as scoped):
- Bare name tokens "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]": word-boundary grep = 0 files; "joneses"/"Joneses" correctly preserved (meta-discussion, not PII); no `[REDACTED]@`/`[REDACTED]-` actor-token forms.
- GitHub handle `[REDACTED]` and stem `[REDACTED]`: literal grep = 0 files. The de-spaced handle-derived slug (`[REDACTED]-audience-profiles`) is gone.
- The "hipster"/"[REDACTED]" substring file counts (20/19) are all either the protected word "Joneses", the meta-discussion of the scrub itself (`<thehipster…` truncation analysis), or the spaced brand (below) — no leak of the literal handle/name tokens.
- The `github.com/<owner>/` URLs that remain (Arize-ai, langchain-ai, TheOctocatX, octocat-operator, etc.) are third-party/placeholder owners, not the operator.
- Regression tests pass (13/13) and genuinely discriminate against the round-1 (case-sensitive, no-stem) state.

The residual gap (CONCERN): The operator's human-readable brand persona "[REDACTED]" / "[REDACTED]" remains in cleartext in ~20 files (e.g. `through [[REDACTED]](https://[REDACTED].substack.com)`, "[REDACTED] persona taxonomy", "[REDACTED] audience profiles"). The scrubbed GitHub handle `[REDACTED]` is exactly this brand with spaces removed, and the operator email is `[REDACTED]`. Anyone reading the corpus reconstructs the already-scrubbed handle and email by concatenation. This is the same operator-identity vector as J-008, only space-separated — the fix scrubs the de-spaced handle and its slug-stem but not the spaced source brand it derives from.

input.rationale: Independently verified both code and committed corpus on branch harden/pass-7 (HEAD fbb9511). The round-2 fix is appropriate: correct root cause (operator-identity PII distinct from email/username), correct layer (_scrub in capture_transcripts.py, applied after the username rule), sound approach (per-token name redaction with trailing \b + re.IGNORECASE; handle+stem redaction derived from git remote/env). Over-redaction is acceptable — sampled "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" contexts are all operator governance references and "Joneses" is correctly preserved. The two in-scope items are clean: grep -riwl "[REDACTED]" = 0 files, grep -ril "[REDACTED]" = 0 files, no [REDACTED]@/[REDACTED]- actor tokens, no standalone "[REDACTED]". Regression tests pass 13/13 and genuinely discriminate — I reproduced that a case-sensitive revert leaves "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" and a no-stem revert leaves "octocatx", exactly what the tests assert against. HOWEVER a closely-adjacent residual operator-identity vector remains: the human-readable brand persona "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" is in cleartext in ~20 files (e.g. "through [[REDACTED]](https://[REDACTED].substack.com)", "[REDACTED] persona taxonomy", "[REDACTED] audience profiles"). The scrubbed GitHub handle "[REDACTED]" is literally this brand de-spaced, and the operator email is [REDACTED] — so a reader trivially reconstructs the already-redacted handle and email by concatenation. This is the same J-008 vector, space-separated; the fix scrubs the de-spaced handle/slug-stem but not the spaced source brand it derives from. Therefore appropriate but not complete.
concerns: Residual operator-identity leak: the brand persona '[REDACTED]' / '[REDACTED]' appears in cleartext across ~20 transcript files (e.g. 'through [[REDACTED]](https://[REDACTED].substack.com)', '[REDACTED] persona taxonomy', '[REDACTED] audience profiles'). The scrubbed GitHub handle '[REDACTED]' is exactly this brand with spaces removed and the operator email is [REDACTED], so the redacted handle/email are trivially reconstructable by concatenation. The handle-stem rule covers de-spaced derivatives ([REDACTED]-audience-profiles) but not the spaced source brand. Fix: add a space-flexible pattern for the brand persona (e.g. derive from the handle by inserting optional separators, or add '[REDACTED]' to ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES/NAMES with whitespace-flexible matching) and re-scrub the corpus.

content.text:
Verification complete. Verdict: CONCERN.

The round-2 case-insensitive name-token + handle-stem fix is appropriate and the specific J-002/J-008 items in scope are clean (bare "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" and the handle/stem "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" all grep to 0 files; tests pass 13/13 and genuinely discriminate against a reverted state). But the corpus is not fully clean: the operator's brand persona "[REDACTED]" / "[REDACTED]" remains in cleartext in ~20 files, and since the scrubbed handle `[REDACTED]` is that brand de-spaced (and the email is `[REDACTED]`), the redacted handle/email are trivially reconstructable. That residual vector is named in the structured output with a concrete remediation.
